Authors: S. Solomon Darnell, Rupert W. Overall, Andrea Guarracino, Vicenza Colonna, Flavia Villani, Erik Garrison, Arun Isaac, Priscilla Muli, Frederick Muriuki Muriithi, Alexander Kabui, Munyoki Kilyungi, Felix Lisso, Adrian Kibet, Brian Muhia, Harm Nijveen, Siamak Yousefi, David Ashbrook, Pengzhi Huang, G. Edward Suh, Muhammad Umar, Christopher Batten, Hao Chen, Saunak Sen, Robert W. Williams, and Pjotr Prins
Study Design: Development and evaluation study; Benchmarking study with mixed methods (automated RAGAS + human evaluation)
Medical Specialty: Systems genetics; Aging; Dementia; Alzheimer's disease; Diabetes
Clinical Task Category: Literature retrieval and summarization; Question-answering system; Knowledge base
Clinician Involvement: Yes - Domain expert evaluation and feedback
Working Model Name: OpenAI GPT-4, GPT-3.5, GPT-4o; FahamuAI "digest" (RAG service)
Medical Specialization: GNQA: domain-specialized for aging, dementia, Alzheimer's, diabetes, and GeneNetwork research
Prompt Engineering: Yes - Prompts used for RAGAS evaluation and question generation
LLM-as-a-Judge Approach: Yes - RAGAS uses GPT for automated evaluation
Judge Specialization: General-purpose LLM adapted for RAG evaluation
Human-in-the-Loop Role: Expert evaluation and feedback; Rating system (thumbs up/down)

Expert Credentials: Domain experts: graduate degree in biology or majored in biology; Citizen scientists: undergraduate level or less, did not major/minor in biology
Robustness Testing: Yes - Hallucination assessment via faithfulness metric; Performance tested across user groups and topics
Dataset Size: Corpus: 3000 publications (1000 GeneNetwork, 1000 aging, 1000 diabetes); Questions: 113 unique human questions (52 domain experts, 61 citizen scientists) + 120 GPT-generated questions

Cross-Domain Testing: Yes - Tested across 3 research topics (GeneNetwork, aging, diabetes) and 2 user groups (domain experts, citizen scientists)
Future Work Suggested: 1) Expand corpus with textbooks and additional publications; 2) Implement open-source LLMs (Llama, Mamba, Olmo); 3) Use open-source RAG engine (R2R); 4) Implement agents for real-time updates; 5) Improve prompting; 6) Address scalability

Jain, Birju Patel, Oluseyi Fayanju, Shreya Shah, Ethan Goh, Dong-han Yao, Brian Soetikno, Eduardo Reis, Sergios Gatidis, Vasu Divi, Robson Capasso, Rachna Saralkar, Chia-Chun Chiang, Jenelle Jindal, Tho Pham, Faraz Ghoddusi, Steven Lin, Albert S. Chiou, Christy Hong, Mohana Roy, Michael F. Gensheimer, Hinesh Patel, Kevin Schulman, Dev Dash, Danton Char, Lance Downing, Francois Grolleau, Kameron Black, Bethel Mieso, Aydin Zahedivash, Wen-wai Yim, Harshita Sharma, Tony Lee, Hannah Kirsch, Jennifer Lee, Nerissa Ambers, Carlene Lugtu, Aditya Sharma, Bilal Mawji, Alex Alekseyev, Vicky Zhou, Vikas Kakkar, Jarrod Helzer, Anurang Revri, Yair Bannett, Roxana Daneshjou, Jonathan Chen, Emily Alsentzer, Keith Morse, Nirmal Ravi, Nima Aghaeepour, Vanessa Kennedy, Akshay Chaudhari, Thomas Wang, Sanmi Koyejo, Matthew P. Lungren, Eric Horvitz, Percy Liang, Mike Pfeffer, Nigam H. Shah
Study Design: Benchmark evaluation study; systematic comparative evaluation
Healthcare Setting: Multiple settings including hospital inpatient, outpatient clinics, emergency department, research settings
Medical Specialty: Multiple specialties (14 specialties involved in validation: Internal Medicine, Radiology, Otolaryngology, Neurology, Family Medicine, Dermatology, Anesthesiology, Radiation Oncology, Emergency Medicine, Ophthalmology, Psychiatry, Pathology, Pediatrics, Nuclear Medicine)
Clinical Task Category: Five categories: Clinical Decision Support, Clinical Note Generation, Patient Communication & Education, Medical Research Assistance, Administration & Workflow
Implementation Stage: Evaluation/benchmarking phase (not deployment or clinical implementation)
Clinician Involvement: Yes - 29 clinicians for taxonomy validation, 20 clinicians for LLM-jury validation
Working Model Name: Nine models evaluated: DeepSeek R1, o3-mini (2025-01-31), Claude 3.7 Sonnet (20250219), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (20241022), GPT-4o (2024-05-13), Gemini 2.0 Flash, GPT-4o mini (2024-07-18), Llama 3.3 Instruct (70B), Gemini 1.5 Pro (001)
Model Family: Multiple families: DeepSeek, OpenAI (GPT-4/o3), Anthropic (Claude), Google (Gemini), Meta (Llama)
Model Size: Llama 3.3 70B explicitly mentioned; other models' parameter counts not specified
Adaptation Method: No adaptation - models used via API in zero-shot or few-shot settings
Prompt Engineering: Yes - standardized prompt templates developed for each benchmark
LLM-as-a-Judge Approach: Yes - LLM-jury approach with three-model panel
Human-in-the-Loop Role: Validation of LLM-jury approach; clinicians rated subset of outputs to validate automated evaluation
Judge Validation Method: Intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC(3,k)) after z-score normalization
Number of Human Experts: 29 clinicians for taxonomy validation; 20 clinicians for evaluation validation
Expert Credentials: Practicing clinicians from 14 medical specialties at 4 institutions (primarily Stanford University, also Mayo Clinic, Oregon Health & Science University, Flourish Research)
Agreement Measure: Intraclass Correlation Coefficient ICC(3,k) with z-scoring
Agreement Score: LLM-jury vs. clinicians: ICC = 0.47 (95% CI: 0.100-0.690); Average clinician-clinician: ICC = 0.43 (95% CI: 0.295-0.585)
Fairness Assessment: Limited - RaceBias benchmark assesses racial bias in model outputs
Robustness Testing: Limited - sensitivity analysis for gold standard quality
Dataset Size: 35 benchmarks total; individual benchmark sizes vary (not all specified)
Data Source: Mixed: MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, Stanford Healthcare EHR data, PubMed, MedQA, public medical datasets
Data Type: Clinical notes, EHR codes, patient-doctor conversations, medical questions, radiology reports, discharge summaries, patient messages, literature abstracts
Geographic Coverage: Primarily United States (Stanford Healthcare, Stanford University based)
Key Limitations: LLM-jury validated on only 2 benchmarks; uneven benchmark distribution (15 of 22 subcategories have only 1 benchmark); 14 datasets not publicly released; limited understanding of poor performance in Administration & Workflow; instance-level rubrics needed
Generalizability: Framework designed to be extensible with open architecture for adding new benchmarks
Cross-Domain Testing: Yes - comprehensive coverage across 5 categories, 22 subcategories, 121 tasks
Deployment Readiness: Not assessed - this is an evaluation framework, not a deployment study
Future Work Suggested: Yes - expanding LLM-jury validation across more benchmarks, developing instance-level rubrics, understanding causes of poor performance in Administration & Workflow, adding more benchmarks to underrepresented subcategories

Title: A Novel Evaluation Benchmark for Medical LLMs: Illuminating Safety and Effectiveness in Clinical Domains
Authors: Shirui Wang, Zhihui Tang, Huaxia Yang, Qiuhong Gong, Tiantian Gu, Hongyang Ma, and 18 additional co-authors
Healthcare Setting: Multiple clinical departments across hospitals in China
Medical Specialty: 26 clinical departments including cardiology, respiratory, neurosurgery, gastroenterology, pediatrics, oncology, etc.
Clinical Task Category: Multiple: clinical decision support, diagnosis, medication safety, guideline adherence, treatment optimization
Implementation Stage: Benchmark evaluation phase - testing models on standardized scenarios
Clinician Involvement: Yes - 32 specialist physicians developed and reviewed benchmark
Working Model Name: Six models tested: DeepSeek-R1-0528, OpenAI-o3 (20250416), Gemini-2.5-Pro (20250506), Qwen3-235B-A22B, Claude-3.7-Sonnet (20250219), MedGPT (MG-0623)
Model Family: Multiple families: DeepSeek, OpenAI, Google (Gemini), Alibaba (Qwen), Anthropic (Claude), Medlinker (MedGPT)
Medical Specialization: MedGPT is domain-specific medical model; others are general-purpose
Adaptation Method: Not specified for evaluation (models used as-is)
Prompt Engineering: Yes - tested with and without structured system prompts
LLM-as-a-Judge Approach: Yes - DeepSeek-R1 used as automated judge
Human-in-the-Loop Role: Expert validation and calibration of automated scoring
Judge Validation Method: Macro-F1 score comparing automated scoring to physician evaluations
Number of Human Experts: 32 specialist physicians for benchmark development; 5 oncologists for judge validation
Expert Credentials: Specialist physicians from major Chinese hospitals across 26 specialties
Agreement Score: DeepSeek-R1 achieved MF1 of 0.601 vs. human consensus baseline of 0.625 (difference: -0.024)
Robustness Testing: Yes - Worst-at-k metric for repeatability evaluation
Data Source: Developed by 32 specialist physicians based on clinical expert consensus
Key Limitations: Limited rare disease coverage, lacks multimodal inputs, single-turn text-only interactions, Chinese-only scenarios, no cross-linguistic validation
Generalizability: Limited - Chinese clinical context only, not validated across languages/countries
Cross-Domain Testing: Yes - 26 clinical departments, 11 priority patient populations
Deployment Readiness: Moderate - identifies significant safety gaps requiring improvement before deployment
Future Work Suggested: Yes - expand to rare diseases and multimodal inputs, multi-turn dialogues, multilingual validation, strengthen drug safety databases

Title: FineRadScore: A Radiology Report Line-by-Line Evaluation Technique Generating Corrections with Severity Scores
Authors: Alyssa Huang, Oishi Banerjee, Kay Wu, Eduardo Pontes Reis, Pranav Rajpurkar
Journal/Venue: Proceedings of Machine Learning Research (PMLR) 252:1–29, 2024 Machine Learning for Healthcare
Study Design: Development and validation study; Benchmarking study with human expert validation
Clinical Task Category: Report quality evaluation; Automated assessment; Error detection and correction
Clinician Involvement: Yes - Radiologists for annotation and validation
Working Model Name: GPT-4 (via Azure OpenAI); Claude-3 Opus (via Anthropic API)
Medical Specialization: FineRadScore: specialized for radiology report evaluation through domain-specific prompting
Prompt Engineering: Yes - Detailed prompt with instructions, error categories, severity ratings
LLM-as-a-Judge Approach: YES - Core methodology of the paper
Judge Specialization: Domain-specialized through prompting for radiology report evaluation
Human-in-the-Loop Role: Validation; Ground truth annotation; Quality assessment
Judge Validation Method: Comparison with radiologist annotations; Multiple validation approaches
Number of Human Experts: 2 radiologists (for ReFiSco-v1); Multiple radiologists for other datasets
Expert Credentials: One board-certified radiologist with 7 years experience; One radiology resident
Agreement Score: Mean absolute error <1 for clinical severity (0.62-0.89 depending on dataset/model)
Robustness Testing: Yes - Tested with shuffled and paraphrased reports
Dataset Size: ReFiSco-v1: 100 studies (200 reports - findings + impressions); ReFiSco-v0: 60 studies; ReXVal: 200 report pairs (40 held-out test set)
Data Source: MIMIC-CXR dataset; ClsGen model outputs; ReFiSco-v0; ReXVal
Data Type: Chest X-ray reports (candidate and ground-truth pairs); Line-by-line annotations with corrections and severity scores
Geographic Coverage: Not Specified (MIMIC-CXR is from US institutions)
Prompt Templates Available: Yes - Complete prompt in Appendix A
Key Limitations: 1) Still validated using automated metrics rather than direct radiologist evaluation of corrected reports; 2) Prone to over-correcting based on stylistic differences; 3) Over-labeling with error categories; 4) Performance degrades with paraphrased reports; 5) Requires both candidate and ground truth (not suitable for RLHF)
Generalizability: Limited to chest X-ray reports; Tested across multiple AI models and report types
Cross-Domain Testing: Yes - Tested across different AI models (ClsGen, X-REM, CXR-RePaiR) and report types (findings vs impressions)
Deployment Readiness: Research/evaluation tool - not ready for clinical deployment
Future Work Suggested: 1) Direct radiologist evaluation of corrected reports; 2) Improve handling of stylistically dissimilar reports; 3) Create dataset using MIMIC-CXR expert reports as ground truth; 4) Collect duplicate annotations for ReFiSco-v1; 5) Better methods to ignore phrasing differences
